exemplar.title: How has Stanley Kubrickâs movie, âThe Shining,â influenced Wes Andersonâs use of mise-en-scene in âThe Grand Budapest Hotelâ?

subject.title: English A Lang & Lit
subject.description:
**IB English A Language & Literature** is a **Group 1** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **the study of language and literature through a wide variety of texts and media from different periods, styles, and cultures**, with a strong focus on **critical analysis of both literary and non-literary texts, examining how language choices create meaning and effect in different contexts**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Text analysis | 1h 15m | 2h 15m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL) |
| **Paper 2** | Comparative essay | 1h 45m | 1h 45m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL)  |
| **HL Essay** | Written assignment | N/A | 1200-1500 words | 25% (HL only) |
| **Individual Oral** | Oral presentation | 15 minutes | 15 minutes | 30% (SL), 20% (HL) |

strengths: The topic is communicated accurately and effectively, with a clear title and opening framing the comparative focus on Kubrickâs influence on Andersonâs mise-en-scÃ¨ne.
The research question is sharply focused and directly connected to the subsequent scene-by-scene analysis.
The plan for close analysis of mise-en-scÃ¨ne elements is clearly signalled, giving the methodology a coherent structure.
weaknesses: The methodology lacks a dedicated section and relies on narrative embedding, reducing transparency of source selection.
Occasional wordiness and broad generalizations in the introduction dilute the scholarly framing.
Source selection is only partially informed, with heavy reliance on non-peer-reviewed material.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission demonstrates a clearly stated and effectively communicated topic and research question (bands 5/6) and a mostly complete methodology (band 4/6). However, weaknesses in transparency of methodological description and critical source selection prevent a top-band score. A score of 4/6 recognizes strong focus and communication while acknowledging these shortcomings.

strengths: Good application of mise-en-scÃ¨ne terminology, with accurate discussions of symmetry, perspective, and color contrast in key scenes.
Insightful connections between composition choices (e.g., empty armchairs and character dominance) reinforce thematic interpretation.
Comparative analyses (e.g., asymmetry vs. symmetry in set design) show nuanced understanding of visual storytelling.
weaknesses: Conceptual errors, such as labeling candlelight as a color, indicate gaps in theoretical precision.
Heavy reliance on popular websites for definitions rather than foundational film theory limits scholarly depth.
Inconsistent citation of lighting sources and informal phrasing reduce the effectiveness of source integration.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows good knowledge of both the topic and subject-specific terminology (band 4/6) but slips into generalities and demonstrates some conceptual misunderstandings. Its application of source material is only partially effective. A score of 3/6 reflects clear understanding tempered by these limitations.

strengths: The bibliography of over twenty sources demonstrates substantial research and engagement with relevant material.
Analysis of pattern and symmetry across both films effectively synthesizes visual motifs and supports the central argument.
The conclusion succinctly synthesizes findings and directly answers the research question, showing clear evaluative judgment.
weaknesses: There is a heavy reliance on popular blogs and magazines rather than peer-reviewed scholarship, limiting academic rigor.
Counter-arguments and study limitations are noted only briefly, with minimal critical evaluation or suggestions for further research.
Several analytical points remain descriptive without fully interrogating alternate readings or deeper thematic significance.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality and relevance are consistently strong (band 7-9), and analysis is mostly well supported (band 7-9), but the work lacks the depth of critical evaluation and scholarly sourcing required for the top band. A score of 9/12 acknowledges good, coherent argumentation alongside missed opportunities for deeper critical interrogation.

strengths: The required structure is present, including title page, table of contents, headings, and bibliography, facilitating navigation.
Figures are captioned and sourced in the bibliography, demonstrating attention to visual evidence attribution.
weaknesses: Citation style is inconsistent and sometimes incomplete, with missing publication details and omitted page numbers.
Abrupt sentence endings and occasional mislabelled figures interrupt the narrative flow and clarity.
Long paragraphs covering multiple points reduce readability; transitional language before figures is often missing.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets basic requirements (band 1-2) but has multiple inconsistencies in citation style, figure labelling, and paragraphing that prevent a higher score. A score of 2/4 reflects adequate structure and formatting with significant room for improvement in consistency and flow.

strandSelections.reasoning: The essay explains that it will use close scene analysis supported by secondary commentary and it provides a structured section plan and word count.  A reasonable number of sources are gathered and a full bibliography is supplied.  However, most sources are popular websites, blogs or magazines rather than peer-reviewed film scholarship, and in-text citations are sporadic.  Thus the methodology is mostly complete but only partially convincing in its evidence of informed, critically selected sources.

Basic layout conventions are met: word count, title, page numbers and a table of contents are provided, and images are captioned.  However, citation style is inconsistent, some image sources are duplicated, and several references lack full publication details, indicating only partial adherence to formal presentation standards.  These shortcomings place the work in the âsome considerations missing or incorrectly appliedâ band.

The bibliography lists over twenty sources and the majority directly address either Kubrick, Anderson, or mise-en-scÃ¨ne.  While academic depth is uneven, the selection is generally relevant to the research question and provides sufficient material for the discussion.  The reliance on popular articles prevents the research from reaching âexcellentâ status, but it is solidly âgoodâ and mostly pertinent.

The studentâs title, subtitle and opening paragraph communicate the topicâKubrickâs influence on Andersonâs mise-en-scÃ¨neâclearly, outlining the key cinematic elements that will be compared (symmetry, colour, set design, etc.).  The reader immediately understands both the cinematic works under investigation and the analytical lens to be applied.  Occasional wordiness aside, the purpose and focus remain evident throughout, satisfying the descriptor for accurate and effective communication.

The discussion culminates in a clear conclusion that synthesises findings, but evaluation of the implications and limitations of the study is brief.  Counter-arguments (e.g., other influences on Anderson or distinctions that weaken the asserted link) are noted yet not explored in depth.  The argument is coherent and generally persuasive, though it lacks the sustained critical interrogation required for the top bands.

Scene comparisons are thoughtfully organised around specific elements (corridors, colour, lighting, etc.), and the student draws logical connections between visual evidence and the claim of influence.  Yet several points remain descriptiveâlisting similarities without fully interrogating their significance or alternative readingsâso conclusions are âmostly supportedâ rather than rigorously argued, aligning with the âgoodâ but not âexcellentâ band.

The essay follows the required EE structureâtitle page, abstract substitute, contents page, introduction, main body with clear headings, conclusion and bibliography.  Headings are logical and facilitate navigation through the argument.  All mandatory elements are present, so the structure is clearly appropriate, though not especially sophisticated.

The student shows good knowledge of mise-en-scÃ¨ne terminology and of the two directorsâ stylistic signatures, supporting points with scene details and still images.  Nonetheless, the understanding sometimes slips into generalities (e.g., âwarm colours = comfortâ) and depends heavily on secondary anecdotes rather than scholarly theory, limiting the depth of insight.  Overall knowledge is clear but its application is only partially effective.

Terminology such as symmetry, blocking, one-point perspective, diegetic/non-diegetic sound and colour palette is used mostly accurately and in context across the essay.  Occasional mis-labelling (e.g., calling candlelight âcolourâ rather than lighting) and some informal phrasing prevent full consistency, but the overall command of subject-specific language meets the descriptor for âmostly accurateâ usage.

The research question is explicitly stated, sharply delimited to one Kubrick film and one Anderson film, and anchored to the single aspect of mise-en-scÃ¨ne.  Scene-by-scene comparison sustains a direct link between the discussion and the question, demonstrating consistent focus.  Minor drift into biographical background does not materially weaken the alignment, so the work fulfils the band description of a clearly stated, focused, and effectively connected RQ.

annotations.comment: Providing the word count meets the formatting requirement and demonstrates awareness of the extended essay guidelines.
The table of contents is comprehensive and clearly structured, facilitating easy navigation through the required EE sections.
Opening paragraphs rely on broad generalizations about âpublic perceptionâ and enumerate similarities descriptively without establishing a scholarly framing or theoretical context.
Heavy reliance on popular blogs and magazine articles rather than peer-reviewed scholarship limits the academic credibility of the research base.
Bibliography ordering mixes general sources with figure captions rather than using a clear alphabetical or thematic arrangement.
The final sentence of this section is incomplete, ending abruptly and interrupting the analytical flow; ensure full sentence construction.
No transitional phrase precedes figure insertion, interrupting narrative continuity; consider adding linking language to guide the reader.
Examples of Tarantino and Wong Kar Wai enrich context but lack explicit linkage back to the research question or comparative analysis framework.
The discussion of Kubrickâs use of symmetry, perspective, and music in multiple films shows good knowledge of topic and effective application of concrete examples.
Labeling candlelight as a âcolourâ demonstrates a conceptual misunderstanding of lighting versus colour terminology in mise-en-scÃ¨ne analysis.
The overview of Andersonâs stylistic traits remains largely descriptive; deeper interrogation of how these elements advance narrative or theme is needed.
The title concisely frames the investigationâs focus on Kubrickâs influence on Andersonâs mise-en-scÃ¨ne, immediately orienting the reader to the comparative film analysis ahead.
The research question is clearly stated, narrowly scoped to two films and the use of mise-en-scÃ¨ne, ensuring precise alignment between inquiry and analysis.
The section header clearly marks the transition into focused mise-en-scÃ¨ne analysis, enhancing the essayâs overall structure.
In-text citations use bracketed names and years inconsistently and omit page numbers, weakening the academic rigor of source attribution.
The final sentence in the introduction ends abruptly, indicating a missing phrase or editing oversight that disrupts the flow.
The student clearly outlines the plan to analyze mise-en-scÃ¨ne elements across scenes, effectively signalling the essayâs methodological approach.
A dedicated methodology section is missing; embedding the approach within narrative reduces transparency of source selection and analytical procedure.
Definition of mise-en-scÃ¨ne relies solely on a popular website without citing foundational film theory texts, limiting theoretical depth of the concept.
Analysis of Figures 1 and 2 asserts symmetry-driven comfort without considering alternate readings, such as symbolic emptiness or narrative subtext.
Linking empty armchairs to character dominance effectively applies mise-en-scÃ¨ne concepts to interpret spatial composition and theme.
The comparison of asymmetrical versus symmetrical set design demonstrates nuanced understanding of how composition shapes viewer emotion.
A single long paragraph covers multiple analytical points; breaking into focused paragraphs would improve clarity and reader engagement.
Discussion of sound in The Shining is thorough but lacks a parallel deep dive into Andersonâs use of music, missing a fully balanced comparison.
Dining hall comparison is introduced clearly and logically follows corridor analysis, maintaining coherent essay structure.
Lighting discussion lacks citations to film theory or design sources, weakening the scholarly underpinnings of claims about divided versus soft spotlight usage.
Analysis of color contrast in snow scenes demonstrates accurate application of color theory associations to thematic interpretation.
Describing foggy atmosphere as âmisplacementâ is unclear and unconventional; clarify terminology or support with scholarly film studies references.
High-angle framing comparison effectively connects camera angle choices to thematic effects of vulnerability and nostalgia.
Discussion of pattern and symmetry across both films synthesizes visual motifs and supports the central argument of influence.
Comparative kitchen scene analysis insightfully highlights parallels in mise-en-scÃ¨ne choices, reinforcing the studyâs central thesis.
The text refers mistakenly to âFigure 2â instead of âFigure 12,â which may confuse readers; verify all figure numbers for accuracy.
The conclusion succinctly synthesizes the analysis and directly answers the research question, demonstrating clear evaluative judgement.
The conclusion omits discussion of study limitations or suggestions for future research, missing an evaluative dimension expected at HL.
The bibliography lists over twenty sources, indicating extensive research and engagement with a range of materials.
Citation entries vary in style (italics, date formats, URL punctuation), undermining consistency and professional presentation.
Figures 9â12 are fully sourced in the bibliography, reflecting attention to visual evidence attribution and scholarly convention.
